topic: A person learns their apartment building is going condo
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy brochure, thick and smelling faintly of new carpet, lay on my kitchen counter like a declaration of war. I traced the outline of the 'Luxury Living' section with a trembling finger. Every time I glanced at the faded, peeling paint on my hallway walls, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. I kept staring at the landlord’s notice tacked to the bulletin board—a stark, black font announcing the transition to condominiums. My hands started shaking as I tried to make coffee; the liquid sloshed over the rim of the mug, staining the counter. I felt a sudden, intense urge to hide under the nearest table, to simply disappear from the sight of the impending change.

[story 2]

"Condo," I repeated, the word tasting like ash on my tongue. I stood by the window, watching the neighboring building’s pristine, modern entrance gleam under the afternoon sun. My shoulders slumped inward, a heavy, defeated posture settling over me. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but my thumb hovered over the call button, paralyzed by a strange, brittle silence. The air in the apartment felt thick, suffocating. I walked over to the living room sofa, sinking into it as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The mail arrived today, and nestled amongst the junk flyers was the official notification. I picked it up with two fingers, my grip surprisingly weak. My breath hitched, a shallow, sharp intake of air that did little to steady my racing heart. I looked around my small living space—the worn armchair, the mismatched curtains—and suddenly everything felt exposed, scrutinized under a harsh, unforgiving light. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over me, making my face feel hot and tight. I dropped the paper onto the floor, the sound echoing too loudly in the sudden quiet, and began methodically stacking books, a frantic, pointless activity to keep my hands busy and my eyes off the door.

I traced the outline of the 'Luxury Living' section with a trembling finger. Every time I glanced at the faded, peeling paint on my hallway walls, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. My hands started shaking as I tried to make coffee; the liquid sloshed over the rim of the mug, staining the counter. I felt a sudden, intense urge to hide under the nearest table, to simply disappear from the sight of the impending change.

"Condo," I repeated, the word tasting like ash on my tongue. I stood by the window, watching the neighboring building’s pristine, modern entrance gleam under the afternoon sun. My shoulders slumped inward, a heavy, defeated posture settling over me. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but my thumb hovered over the call button, paralyzed by a strange, brittle silence. The air in the apartment felt thick, suffocating. I walked over to the living room sofa, sinking into it as if gravity had suddenly increased tenfold, wishing the floor would simply swallow me whole.

The mail arrived today, and nestled amongst the junk flyers was the official notification. I picked it up with two fingers, my grip surprisingly weak. My breath hitched, a shallow, sharp intake of air that did little to steady my racing heart. I looked around my small living space—the worn armchair, the mismatched curtains—and suddenly everything felt exposed, scrutinized under a harsh, unforgiving light. A wave of intense awkwardness washed over me, making my face feel hot and tight. I dropped the paper onto the floor, the sound echoing too loudly in the sudden quiet, and began methodically stacking books, a frantic, pointless activity to keep my hands busy and my eyes off the door.
